messages.content: edit: The organization's ability to acquire, deploy, and leverage its IT resources in combination with other resources and capabilities to support and enhance its distinctive competencies to achieve its business goals

The ability of an organization to effectively acquire, deploy, and utilize its IT resources in conjunction with other resources and capabilities to strengthen and enhance its unique strengths and competencies, ultimately resulting in the achievement of its business objectives.
edit: A high-level map and detailed description of information and technology needed to meet business objectives
A comprehensive overview and detailed explanation of the necessary information and technology resources required to achieve business objectives.
edit: The ability of IT staff to consistently solve business problems and addresses opportunities through IT
The capacity of IT personnel to consistently identify solutions for business challenges and capitalize on opportunities through the strategic use of IT.
edit: The ability of an organization to set valuable relationship between the IT unit and business units

The ability of an organization to establish and maintain a productive relationship between the IT department and other business units, ultimately resulting in the efficient and effective use of IT resources to achieve business goals.
Strategic alignment refers to the application of IT in a timely and appropriate manner, in accordance with existing business strategies, goals and needs

Yes, that is correct. Strategic alignment in the context of IT refers to the alignment of IT initiatives and investments with the overall business strategy and objectives of the organization. It involves making sure that IT is implemented in a timely and appropriate manner to support the business goals and needs of the organization. This ensures that all IT activities are in harmony with the overall business strategy, resulting in increased efficiency, effectiveness, and ultimately the achievement of business objectives.
edit: The degree to which to the application of IT in a timely and appropriate manner, in accordance with existing business strategies, goals and needs
The extent to which IT is applied in a timely and appropriate manner, in accordance with the existing business strategies, goals and needs of an organization.

edit: First, we developed a questionnaire instrument to measure the constructs. As shown in Appendix A, we adapted 20 items as measures of the physical IT infrastructure and IT capabilities dimensions from previous studies (e.g., Fink & Neumann, 2009; Zhang, 2005; Jorfi et al., 2017). Four items adapted based on Fink & Neumann (2009) and Zhang (2005) operationalize strategic alignment. Five items adapted from Ly, (2023) and Nasiri et al.(2020), measured the digital transformation. A seven-point Likert scale was used for the items, ranging from "strongly disagree" to "strongly agree". Second, the initial instrument was pre-tested in two interviews with two hospital IT managers and three academics and then pilot-tested, resulting in 14 returned questionnaires. Reliability analysis of the scales revealed a satisfactory Cronbach's alpha, ranging from 0.735 to 0.886. Third, IT managers (or similar titles) of hospitals in Iran were selected as the target population because they have a combined managerial/professional perspective; our approach was consistent with previous IS studies (e.g., Ness, 2005, Mohammed et al., 2016). Therefore, the final questionnaire was distributed among the sampling frame and 124 questionnaires were returned.

To measure the constructs in our study, we developed a questionnaire instrument. We adapted 20 items from previous studies (e.g., Fink & Neumann, 2009; Zhang, 2005; Jorfi et al., 2017) to measure the physical IT infrastructure and IT capabilities dimensions. Strategic alignment was operationalized with four items adapted from Fink & Neumann (2009) and Zhang (2005), while five items adapted from Ly (2023) and Nasiri et al. (2020) measured digital transformation. A seven-point Likert scale was used for all items, with responses ranging from "strongly disagree" to "strongly agree."

The initial instrument was pre-tested in two interviews with two hospital IT managers and three academics, and then pilot-tested with 14 returned questionnaires. Reliability analysis of the scales revealed a satisfactory Cronbach's alpha, ranging from 0.735 to 0.886.

We selected IT managers (or similar titles) of hospitals in Iran as the target population for the study, as they had a combined managerial and professional perspective. This approach was consistent with previous IS studies (e.g., Ness, 2005; Mohammed et al., 2016). We distributed the final questionnaire among the sampling frame and received 124 completed questionnaires.
edit: Data were analyzed using SPSS (version 27) and Smart PLS (version 3). Data analysis was performed in the following steps.
The data collected for our study were analyzed using SPSS (version 27) and Smart PLS (version 3). The data analysis process consisted of the following steps.

edit: First, using the results of the boxplots in SPSS, nine outliers were identified and removed from the analysis. Further analyses were performed on the remaining 115 questionnaires. Second, the normality of the data was analyzed, although a normal distribution is not required for PLS analysis. The results of all constructs in the analysis of skewness (from -.028 to .410) and kurtosis (from -.827 to .651) ranged from -2 to +2, demonstrating the normality of the data.

Initially, we identified nine outliers using boxplots in SPSS and excluded them from the analysis. The remaining 115 questionnaires were considered for analysis. The normality of the data was analyzed as a secondary step, though it is not required for PLS analysis. 

The skewness (ranging from -.028 to .410) and kurtosis (ranging from -.827 to .651) of all constructs in the analysis were evaluated and ranged from -2 to +2. This indicates that the data were normally distributed and do not require further normalization before conducting the analysis.

edit: The demographic characteristics of participating hospitals were examined in terms of size and type (see Table 2), which were included in the model as control variables. Size was tested using three categories, including small, medium, and large. Type was also tested with respect to public and private hospitals.

The demographic features of the participating hospitals were analyzed concerning their size and type (refer to Table 2). These factors were also incorporated into the model as control variables. The size of the hospitals was assessed using three categories, comprising small, medium, and large. On the other hand, the type of the hospitals was scrutinized based on whether they were public or private.
edit: *Hospitals with less than 100 beds are not included.
*Hospitals with fewer than 100 beds were excluded from the study.

edit: 	Unexpectedly, hospital size and type are not confirmed as control variables . A plausible explanation for size is the potential trade-off that typically exists between the agility of smaller hospitals and the capabilities of larger hospitals. In addition, a possible explanation for type of hospital is the trade-off between the capabilities of private hospitals and the timeliness of public hospitals, most of which are educational facilities and closely linked to the academic environment.

Unexpectedly, the analysis did not confirm hospital size and type as significant control variables in our study. One possible explanation for the lack of significance of hospital size is the tension that often exists between the agility of smaller hospitals and the more significant capabilities and resources of larger hospitals. Similarly, the lack of significance for the hospital type could be explained by the trade-off between the operational efficiency of private hospitals and the responsiveness of public hospitals, many of which have academic affiliations and are closely linked to the academic environment.

improve: 	Our empirical analysis has also shown that strategic alignment and digital transformation depend on the dimensions of IT capabilities, but not on the physical IT infrastructure. This is consistent with the view that physical IT infrastructures are easier to acquire and tradable and not firm-specific. Instead, IT capabilities are firm-specific and are often developed over a long period of time. Thus, physical IT infrastructure and IT capabilities do not contribute equally to digital transformation and strategic alignment.

Our empirical analysis has revealed that the dimensions of IT capabilities significantly impact strategic alignment and digital transformation, whereas the physical IT infrastructure does not play a significant role. This finding is consistent with the view that physical IT infrastructures are more easily acquired and are tradable, whereas IT capabilities are firm-specific and developed over a more extended period. Therefore, physical IT infrastructure and IT capabilities do not equally contribute to digital transformation and strategic alignment.
improve more

Our empirical analysis has discovered that the dimensions of IT capabilities have a more substantial impact on achieving strategic alignment and digital transformation, while physical IT infrastructure does not significantly contribute to these goals. This finding aligns with the perception that physical IT infrastructure is tradable and more comfortable to acquire, whereas IT capabilities are unique to each firm and develop over time. 

Hence, the importance of physical IT infrastructure and IT capabilities in achieving digital transformation and strategic alignment is not equal. Organizations must develop and leverage their IT capabilities to achieve strategic alignment and digital transformation, rather than relying solely on their physical IT infrastructure. This requires a long-term approach to IT capability development and careful management of IT resources to ensure alignment with business objectives.
